title: node:test, Fixtures, Compile-Negative Cases, Faults, and Mutation Evidence · Scenario Checkpoint
summary: A release reviewer compares near-miss TypeScript decisions for node:test, fixtures, compile-negative cases, faults, and mutation evidence and accepts only compiler plus runtime changed-case evidence, explicit non-claims, and named network, DNS, browser, accessibility, security, load, legal-review, and production transfer gates. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for risk-layered deterministic crawler test system. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory…
objectives: Build original accepted, malformed, adversarial, boundary, changed, Unicode, duplicate, inaccessible, and privacy-sensitive fixtures with provenance.
Test URL, robots, sitemap, state, scheduling, extraction, dedup, report, and recovery invariants with deterministic clocks and schedules.
Use expected TypeScript errors to prove invalid state, unsafe narrowing, mutable values, and missing exhaustive cases are rejected.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Fixture Taxonomy · changed case · Quiz Fixture Taxonomy
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz TypeScript team handles case 8239 during a hostile redirect, stream, and checkpoint drill. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove original fixtures, pure models, compile-negative tests, loopback faults, and mutation detection; browser TypeScript remains deterministic and free of Node, network, filesystem, browser, and host effects; then reorder one frontier, p-limit, Promise, AbortSignal, stream, retry, checkpoint, or shutdown event and prove ownership. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates.
